items.title: Customer segmentation without fixing k first
items.scenario: A subscription business has RFM features (recency, frequency, monetary value) for 800 customers. Analysts run agglomerative clustering with Ward linkage on standardized features and inspect the dendrogram before deciding whether to operate with 3 broad segments or 6 finer ones for campaigns.
items.explanation: The dendrogram shows when natural merges occur; marketing can choose a cut height that balances actionable segment size with interpretability, without re-fitting as k changes.

items.title: Gene expression heatmaps and sample trees
items.scenario: In bioinformatics, rows are genes and columns are tissue samples. Hierarchical clustering on samples produces a dendrogram reordered beside a heatmap, revealing which samples cluster by treatment or disease state.
items.explanation: The tree order makes patterns visible in heatmaps; biologists often cut the sample dendrogram to define patient subgroups for downstream tests.

items.title: Document taxonomy for a small knowledge base
items.scenario: A team embeds 200 internal wiki pages with a sentence encoder, then clusters embedding vectors with average linkage. They cut the tree at a height that yields ~12 topic folders for navigation.
items.explanation: Hierarchical structure mirrors how humans organize nested topics; cosine or Euclidean distance on embeddings plus linkage choice determines which pages merge first.
